walkthrough.title: Unfold the Map Board
walkthrough.text: Let's set up Barrage. First, unfold the main game board and place it in the center of the table. The board shows an Alpine mountain landscape divided into basins connected by river channels. Water flows downhill from the top basins to the bottom, and this is where you will build your hydroelectric empire.

walkthrough.title: Place the Round Marker
walkthrough.text: Let's find the round track along the edge of the board. Place the round marker on space one. Barrage is played over exactly five rounds, so this track will move once per round to keep everyone oriented.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Energy Track
walkthrough.text: Let's locate the energy track running along the board. Each player will place a marker at the zero position on this track. The energy track measures how much energy you have produced throughout the game, and your position on it determines both turn order and end-game scoring.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Water Drops
walkthrough.text: Let's gather all the blue water drop tokens and place them in a supply near the board. During each round, water drops are placed into the headwater basins at the top of the map. These drops are the lifeblood of your energy production.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Contract Tiles
walkthrough.text: Let's find all the contract tiles and sort them by color into separate stacks. Each contract shows an energy requirement and a reward. Place the contract stacks face up near the board so everyone can see what is available to claim.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Technology Tiles
walkthrough.text: Let's set up the technology tile area. Sort the technology tiles by type and place them in their designated spots on the board. These tiles give you powerful upgrades and special abilities when acquired during the game.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Objective Tiles
walkthrough.text: Let's select the objective tiles for this game. Draw three objective tiles at random and place them face up near the board. These tiles define the bonus scoring conditions for end-of-game points. Everyone should read them carefully before the game begins.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Company
walkthrough.text: Each player chooses one of the four companies and takes the matching company board. Each company has a unique color and a slightly different starting setup. Take the company board and place it in front of you.

walkthrough.title: Take Your Construction Wheel
walkthrough.text: Let's grab the construction wheel for your company. This is the cardboard wheel mechanism attached to your company board. It rotates each round and controls when your spent machinery returns to you. Make sure the wheel is set to its starting position as shown in the rulebook.

walkthrough.title: Collect Your Structures
walkthrough.text: Let's gather all the wooden structures in your company color. You get dams in three sizes, conduit pieces, and powerhouse tokens. Place them on the designated spaces on your company board. As you build, you will move pieces from your board to the map.

walkthrough.title: Stock Your Machinery
walkthrough.text: Let's take your starting supply of excavators and concrete mixers. These are the construction resources you spend to build structures. Place them in the available machinery area on your company board. You will need to manage these carefully because spent machinery goes into the construction wheel.

walkthrough.title: Take Your Engineers
walkthrough.text: Let's collect your engineer workers. The number of engineers you receive depends on player count. These are the workers you will place on action spaces during each round. Place them near your company board.

walkthrough.title: Select Your Executive Officer
walkthrough.text: Each player selects one executive officer tile. Executive officers give your company a unique asymmetric power for the entire game. Read through the available officers and choose one that matches the strategy you want to pursue. Place it next to your company board.

walkthrough.title: Receive Starting Credits
walkthrough.text: Each player receives their starting credits as shown on their company board. Credits are used as an additional construction cost and for certain action spaces. Place your credit tokens near your company board.

walkthrough.title: Place Neutral Dams
walkthrough.text: For games with fewer than four players, place neutral dam tokens in certain basins as indicated in the rulebook. These dams block those basins from being used and ensure the map scales properly for your player count.

walkthrough.title: Determine Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Let's determine the starting turn order. The player who most recently visited a dam or hydroelectric plant goes first. If nobody has, choose randomly. Place turn order markers accordingly. The player lowest on the energy track always goes first, so this initial order will shift quickly.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Barrage is played over five rounds. In each round, players take turns placing engineers on action spaces to build infrastructure, produce energy, and fulfill contracts. The player who generates the most victory points through energy production, contracts, and bonuses wins the game.

walkthrough.title: Round Structure
walkthrough.text: Each round follows six phases in order. First is water replenishment, where new water drops enter the headwater basins. Second is income, where players receive credits and possibly other bonuses. Third is the action phase, where players take turns placing engineers. Fourth is water flow, where water moves downstream. Fifth is scoring based on the round. Sixth is the construction wheel advance.

walkthrough.title: Water Replenishment
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, new water drops are placed into the headwater basins at the top of the map. The number and placement of drops is shown on the round track. This water is what everyone will compete to capture and use for energy production.

walkthrough.title: Income Phase
walkthrough.text: During income, each player collects credits and any other bonuses shown on their company board. As you build structures and remove them from your company board, you reveal income symbols underneath. The more you build, the more income you generate each round.

walkthrough.title: Action Phase Overview
walkthrough.text: The action phase is the heart of each round. Starting with the player lowest on the energy track, players take turns placing one or more engineers on an available action space. After placing, you immediately perform that action. Play continues around the table until all players have passed.

walkthrough.title: Building Dams
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about dams. To build a dam, place engineers on a construction action space and pay the required excavators, concrete mixers, and credits. Dams come in three levels. A level one dam holds one water drop. A level two dam holds two drops, and a level three holds three. You can also elevate an existing dam by building on top of it.

walkthrough.title: Building Conduits
walkthrough.text: Conduits are the channels that connect a dam to a powerhouse. To build a conduit, pay the required machinery and credits at a construction action space. Conduits have production values printed on them. The higher the production value, the more energy each water drop generates when flowing through that conduit.

walkthrough.title: Building Powerhouses
walkthrough.text: Powerhouses are placed in designated spots on the map. They convert water flowing through conduits into energy. You need all three structures working together: a dam to hold water, a conduit to channel it, and a powerhouse to convert it. Without a complete network, you cannot produce energy.

walkthrough.title: Construction Costs and Machinery
walkthrough.text: Every structure costs a combination of excavators, concrete mixers, and credits. Here is the critical part: when you spend excavators and mixers, they do not go back to your supply. Instead, they go into your construction wheel. They will not return to your available supply until the wheel advances, which takes two to three rounds.

walkthrough.title: The Construction Wheel
walkthrough.text: Let's understand the construction wheel. It has multiple segments. When you spend machinery, you place it in the topmost open segment. At the end of each round, the wheel rotates one segment. Machinery falls out of the bottom and returns to your available supply. This means you must plan ahead because building now depletes your machinery for future rounds.

walkthrough.title: Producing Energy
walkthrough.text: To produce energy, place engineers on the production action space and activate one of your completed dam-conduit-powerhouse networks. Choose how many water drops to release from your dam. Each drop flows through the conduit and generates energy equal to the conduit's production value. Your marker moves up the energy track by that total amount.

walkthrough.title: Water Flows Downstream
walkthrough.text: This is a crucial rule that catches new players off guard. After you produce energy, the water drops you used do not disappear. They flow downstream into the next lower basin on the map. If an opponent has a dam in that lower basin, they capture your used water. This means your production can directly fuel your opponents.

walkthrough.title: Turn Order and the Energy Track
walkthrough.text: Turn order is determined by position on the energy track. The player lowest on the track goes first each round. This is a catch-up mechanism. If you are behind in production, you get to act first and claim the best action spaces. If you are leading, you go last and may find key spaces already taken.

walkthrough.title: Fulfilling Contracts
walkthrough.text: Contracts are tiles that reward you for producing a specific amount of energy in a single activation. Before you produce, you can commit a contract from your hand. If your production meets or exceeds the contract's energy requirement, you fulfill it and receive victory points plus a bonus. Contracts are a major source of scoring.

walkthrough.title: Acquiring Technology Tiles
walkthrough.text: Technology tiles provide permanent upgrades and special abilities. You acquire them by placing engineers on a technology action space and paying the required cost. Technologies can improve your construction efficiency, give you extra production bonuses, or provide new strategic options. Choose technologies that complement your overall plan.

walkthrough.title: External Work Action
walkthrough.text: If you cannot or do not want to build your own structures, you can place engineers on the external work action spaces. These let you contribute to a shared external project in exchange for immediate victory points or resources. External work is useful when your machinery is locked in the wheel.

walkthrough.title: Water Management Action
walkthrough.text: Some action spaces let you manipulate water on the map. You can place a water drop into a basin where you have a dam, or move water between connected basins. Controlling the flow of water is essential to ensuring your dams stay full while your opponents' remain empty.

walkthrough.title: Passing and Round End
walkthrough.text: When you have no more engineers to place or choose not to place any more, you pass. Once all players have passed, the action phase ends. Water in basins without dams flows downstream and off the map. Then the construction wheels advance one segment, returning previously spent machinery to players.

walkthrough.title: Scoring During the Game
walkthrough.text: At the end of certain rounds, bonus scoring occurs based on the objective tiles. These objectives reward things like having the most dams, the longest conduit network, or the most powerhouses in a particular region. Check the objective tiles at the start of the game to align your building strategy.

walkthrough.title: End-Game Scoring
walkthrough.text: After five rounds, final scoring takes place. Players earn victory points from their position on the energy track, fulfilled contracts, objective tile bonuses, building bonuses revealed on their company board, and remaining credits. The player with the most total victory points wins. Tiebreaker goes to the player higher on the energy track.

bullets: 5 rounds of worker placement in an Alpine hydroelectric setting
Build dams, conduits, powerhouses → produce energy → fulfill contracts
Most VP at game end wins

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Alps
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through a practice game of Barrage with four players. Each player controls a different company competing to harness Alpine waterways for hydroelectric power. We will follow along as the players build dams, lay conduits, construct powerhouses, and race to produce energy.

walkthrough.title: Round One Water Arrives
walkthrough.text: Let's begin round one. During water replenishment, new water drops are placed into the headwater basins at the top of the map. Everyone eyes these drops because they are the fuel for energy production. The player who captures them with well-positioned dams will have a huge advantage.

walkthrough.title: Player One Builds a Dam
walkthrough.text: Player one places two engineers on a construction action space and pays three excavators, two concrete mixers, and two credits to build a level two dam in a prime headwater basin. That dam can now hold two water drops. All five of those spent machinery pieces go into their construction wheel and will not come back for two rounds.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Builds Downstream
walkthrough.text: Player two places engineers and builds a level one dam in the basin directly below player one's dam. This is a clever move. Any water that player one produces energy with will flow downstream into player two's dam. Player two is setting up to capture recycled water without spending a single drop.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Downstream Positioning
walkthrough.text: Let's stop and highlight what player two just did. In Barrage, used water flows downhill. If you only build in the headwater basins, you get water first but your opponents below capture everything you release. Smart players build downstream dams to harvest water that others have already used. Always consider who sits below your dams before producing.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Lays a Conduit
walkthrough.text: Player three already placed a dam last round and now lays a conduit connecting that dam to a powerhouse slot. The conduit has a production value of three, meaning each water drop that flows through it will generate three energy. Player three pays two excavators and one mixer for the conduit.

walkthrough.title: Player Four Grabs a Contract
walkthrough.text: Player four takes an action to pick up a contract tile requiring six energy to fulfill. The contract offers four victory points and a bonus excavator. Having a contract in hand before producing is essential because you must commit the contract before your production action.

walkthrough.title: Machinery Running Low
walkthrough.text: Let's check in on player one. After that big dam construction, they have only one excavator and one mixer left in their available supply. The rest are locked in the construction wheel. Player one wanted to also build a conduit this round, but cannot afford it. They will have to wait for the wheel to return their machinery.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: No Recycling Plan
walkthrough.text: Let's pause and talk about player one's mistake. They spent five pieces of machinery on a single dam without considering how long it would take to get them back. In Barrage, machinery is your most constrained resource. You must plan two to three rounds ahead and stagger your builds so the wheel returns machinery in time for your next project.

walkthrough.title: Round Two Production
walkthrough.text: Let's move to round two. Player three has a complete network now: dam with two water drops, conduit with production value three, and a powerhouse. They place engineers on the production action space and release both drops. That is two drops times three production, equaling six energy. Their marker jumps six spaces on the energy track.

walkthrough.title: Contract Fulfillment
walkthrough.text: Before producing, player three committed a contract requiring six energy. Their production of six meets the requirement exactly. They flip the contract face down and earn three victory points plus a bonus credit. Always pick up contracts before you produce so you do not waste a high-value activation.

walkthrough.title: Water Captured Downstream
walkthrough.text: Now those two water drops player three just used flow downstream. They land in a basin where player four has a dam. Player four did not spend any actions getting that water. They just waited in the right position and inherited it for free. This is the power of downstream positioning in action.

walkthrough.title: Wasted Production Mistake
walkthrough.text: Meanwhile, player two produces four energy from their dam. But player two has no contract that matches four energy. They get the energy track movement, but miss out on bonus victory points and resources. Let's learn from this: never produce energy without a matching contract in hand if you can help it. Every contractless activation is wasted potential.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Have a Contract Ready
walkthrough.text: Contracts are not optional side scoring. They are a core part of your point engine. Before spending an action on production, make sure you have a contract whose energy requirement you can meet or exceed. If you do not have one, spend your turn picking up a contract first and produce next round.

walkthrough.title: Technology Tile Acquisition
walkthrough.text: Let's watch player one recover from their machinery shortage. They spend an action to acquire a technology tile that reduces the excavator cost of future conduit builds by one. This is a smart pivot. Instead of sitting idle waiting for the wheel, they invest in an upgrade that will make every future build cheaper.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Construction Wheel Returns
walkthrough.text: At the end of round three, construction wheels rotate again. Player one finally gets back those five pieces of machinery they spent in round one. Now they can build aggressively again. Let's notice how two full rounds passed with limited construction. Staggering your builds across rounds prevents this kind of drought.

walkthrough.title: The Energy Track Race
walkthrough.text: By round four, the energy track tells the story. Player three leads with 14 energy, but that means they go last in turn order. Player one is at six energy and gets to act first, claiming the best action spaces. The catch-up mechanism keeps the game competitive because the leader always picks last.

walkthrough.title: Elevating a Dam
walkthrough.text: Let's watch player four elevate their level one dam to level two. This costs machinery and credits but lets the dam hold an additional water drop. Elevating is often more efficient than building a new dam from scratch because you already have the basin position secured.

walkthrough.title: Round Five Final Push
walkthrough.text: It is the final round. Everyone produces as much energy as possible, fulfills their remaining contracts, and races up the energy track. Player one chains two productions back to back. Player three fulfills their highest-value contract. Player four captures the last available water from an opponent's production run.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Let's tally the scores. Players add energy track points, fulfilled contract points, objective tile bonuses, building bonuses revealed on company boards, and remaining credits. Player three wins with a combination of strong contracts and efficient production. Player two finishes second thanks to aggressive downstream water capture.

walkthrough.title: Key Takeaways
walkthrough.text: Let's review the three big lessons. First, plan your machinery spending two to three rounds ahead so the construction wheel returns resources when you need them. Second, position dams downstream to capture water that opponents release. Third, always have a contract ready before producing energy so no activation goes to waste.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common pitfalls to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to structures, actions, and the water system.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for structures and actions
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, let's cover the most common mistakes new players make. First, spending all your machinery on one big build and having nothing left for two rounds. Second, ignoring downstream positioning so opponents capture all your used water. Third, producing energy without a matching contract in hand. Fourth, neglecting the construction wheel timing. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: Machinery Management
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about the most important skill in Barrage: managing your excavators and mixers. Every piece you spend disappears for two to three rounds. Before building anything, count what you will have left and whether that is enough for your next planned build. Stagger your construction across rounds so the wheel returns machinery just in time. A player who builds every round at moderate cost will outperform a player who builds big once and sits idle twice.

walkthrough.title: Downstream Water Strategy
walkthrough.text: Let's think about water flow as a strategic advantage. Building dams in basins below your opponents means you capture their used water for free. This is especially powerful in the mid and late game when headwater drops become scarce. If you see an opponent investing heavily in a headwater basin, consider building directly below them to harvest every drop they release.

walkthrough.title: Contract Timing
walkthrough.text: Let's plan our contract strategy carefully. Always pick up a contract before producing energy. The ideal sequence is to grab a contract one turn, then produce energy to fulfill it the next. Match the contract's energy requirement as closely as possible to your production capacity. Producing eight energy to fulfill a four-energy contract wastes half your output. Aim for contracts that match your network's output.

walkthrough.title: The Energy Track Trade-Off
walkthrough.text: Let's remember that the energy track is a double-edged sword. Advancing earns you points but pushes you later in turn order. Sometimes it is better to delay production by one round to maintain first-player advantage and grab a critical action space. Conversely, if the action spaces you need are not contested, produce early and bank those points.

walkthrough.title: Build Complete Networks Early
walkthrough.text: Let's prioritize completing at least one dam-conduit-powerhouse network as quickly as possible. A half-built network generates zero energy. Many new players spread their construction across multiple basins and end up with no complete network by round two. Focus your first three to four builds on completing one network, then expand from there.

walkthrough.title: Technology Tile Value
walkthrough.text: Let's not overlook technology tiles. Acquiring the right technology early can compound your advantage over the entire game. A tile that reduces construction costs by one excavator saves you that excavator on every future build. That is five or six free excavators over the course of the game. Prioritize technologies that reduce costs or increase production over one-time bonuses.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Objective Tiles
walkthrough.text: Let's read the three objective tiles at the start of every game and let them guide your building plan. If an objective rewards having the most conduits, focus your construction on conduit builds. If it rewards powerhouses in a specific region, prioritize building there. Objectives can swing ten or more victory points, so aligning your strategy with them is always worthwhile.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Spending all machinery on one build = idle for 2 rounds
Ignoring downstream positioning = opponents capture your water
Producing without a contract = wasted VP potential
Neglecting wheel timing = construction droughts

summary.title: Machinery Management
bullets: Count remaining machinery before every build
Stagger construction so wheel returns pieces on schedule
Moderate builds every round > one massive build then idle
Machinery is the #1 bottleneck in Barrage

summary.title: Downstream Water Strategy
bullets: Build dams below opponents to capture their used water for free
Especially powerful mid/late game when headwater drops are scarce
Opponent invests in headwater → build directly below them

summary.title: Contract Timing
bullets: Grab contract → produce next turn to fulfill it
Match contract energy requirement to your network's output
Overproducing wastes energy; underproducing fails the contract
Contracts are core scoring, not side objectives

summary.title: The Energy Track Trade-Off
bullets: Advancing = VP but pushes you later in turn order
Delay production to keep first-player advantage when needed
Produce early when key action spaces are uncontested

summary.title: Build Complete Networks Early
bullets: Half-built network = 0 energy; complete one ASAP
Focus first 3-4 builds on one dam+conduit+powerhouse
Expand to second network after the first is operational

summary.title: Technology Tile Value
bullets: Cost-reduction techs compound over the entire game
-1 excavator per build = 5-6 free excavators over 5 rounds
Prioritize repeatable bonuses over one-time effects

summary.title: Watch the Objective Tiles
bullets: Read all 3 objectives at game start — guide your building plan
Objectives can swing 10+ VP
Align strategy with objectives: most conduits, regional presence, etc.

walkthrough.title: Water Monopoly Strategy
walkthrough.text: The most dominant strategy in Barrage is controlling the water supply. Build dams in multiple basins along a single river channel so that water flows from your top dam to your bottom dam. When you produce energy from the top, the water drops into your second dam. You produce again from that dam, and the water drops into your third. One set of water drops generates energy three times for you and zero times for opponents.

walkthrough.title: Construction Wheel Optimization
walkthrough.text: Advanced players time their construction wheel like clockwork. The key insight is that the wheel has segments of different sizes. Place your most expensive machinery costs in the largest segment so more pieces return at once. Alternate big builds with small builds to create a steady flow of returning machinery. Never build maximum in consecutive rounds unless your wheel timing specifically supports it.

walkthrough.title: Conduit Value Maximization
walkthrough.text: Your conduit's production value is the single biggest multiplier in the game. A conduit worth two produces two energy per drop, while a conduit worth four produces four energy per drop. That is double the output from the same water. Invest heavily in high-value conduits even if they cost more machinery. The return on investment over multiple productions dwarfs the extra construction cost.

walkthrough.title: Contract Chaining
walkthrough.text: The strongest scoring players chain contracts across rounds. Pick up a contract that matches your current production capacity. Produce and fulfill it. Use the contract's bonus resources to build toward a higher-capacity network. Then pick up a larger contract and fulfill that one. Each fulfilled contract funds the next, creating an escalating scoring engine that accelerates every round.

walkthrough.title: Executive Officer Exploitation
walkthrough.text: Each executive officer breaks the normal rules in a specific way. Advanced players build their entire strategy around their officer's unique power. If your officer reduces dam costs, build dams aggressively in every basin. If your officer grants bonus production, focus on completing networks quickly. The officer should define your strategy, not supplement it.

walkthrough.title: Opponent Denial
walkthrough.text: In a tight game, preventing opponents from scoring is as valuable as scoring yourself. Build a powerhouse in a slot an opponent needs to block their network completion. Place a dam in a basin they were targeting. Take the contract they clearly need before they can grab it. Denial plays are especially powerful in rounds three and four when competition for remaining board positions is fierce.

walkthrough.title: Turn Order Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Advanced players deliberately stay low on the energy track in early rounds to maintain first-player advantage. They use these early turns to claim the best building spots and action spaces while opponents are locked out. Then in round four or five, they unleash massive production runs to rocket up the energy track when turn order matters less. Timing your scoring surge for the final rounds is a hallmark of expert play.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Credit Conversion
walkthrough.text: Credits convert to victory points at the end of the game, but their value during the game is even higher because they enable construction. In the final round, stop building and hoard credits if your networks are already producing. Every credit you save is a victory point, and you no longer need to spend them on construction. Time your last build for round four so round five is pure production and credit banking.

summary.title: Water Monopoly Strategy
bullets: Build dams along a single river channel (top to bottom)
Water flows from your top dam → mid dam → bottom dam
One set of drops generates energy 3x for you, 0x for opponents
Requires heavy early investment but dominates mid/late game

summary.title: Construction Wheel Optimization
bullets: Time builds to wheel segment sizes for optimal returns
Alternate big + small builds for steady machinery flow
Never max-build consecutive rounds without wheel support
Advanced players plan wheel timing 3 rounds ahead

summary.title: Conduit Value Maximization
bullets: Conduit production value = biggest energy multiplier
Value 4 conduit = 2x output vs value 2 conduit (same water)
Extra construction cost is repaid over multiple productions
Prioritize high-value conduits over other structures

summary.title: Contract Chaining
bullets: Fulfill contract → use bonus to build better network → bigger contract
Creates escalating scoring engine across rounds
Match contract energy to current production capacity
Each fulfilled contract funds the next upgrade

summary.title: Executive Officer Exploitation
bullets: Build entire strategy around officer's unique power
Cost-reduction officers → build aggressively in every basin
Production-bonus officers → complete networks ASAP
Officer defines strategy, not supplements it

summary.title: Opponent Denial
bullets: Block powerhouse slots opponents need
Claim basins opponents are targeting for dams
Take contracts opponents clearly need
Most impactful in rounds 3-4 when board gets tight

summary.title: Turn Order Manipulation
bullets: Stay low on energy track early → first-player advantage
Claim best building spots and action spaces while others are locked out
Unleash massive production in rounds 4-5 when turn order matters less
Timing scoring surge for final rounds = expert play

summary.title: Endgame Credit Conversion
bullets: Credits = VP at game end; stop building in round 5 if networks are set
Hoard credits in final round for pure VP
Last build in round 4 → round 5 = production + credit banking
Every unspent credit = 1 VP

entries.definition: Structure placed in a basin to hold water drops. Comes in 3 levels: Level 1 holds 1 drop, Level 2 holds 2, Level 3 holds 3. Can be elevated from a lower level to a higher one.
Structure connecting a dam to a powerhouse. Has a printed production value that determines energy generated per water drop flowing through it. Higher values cost more machinery.
Structure placed in designated map slots that converts water flowing through conduits into energy. Required to complete a production network.
Smallest dam. Holds 1 water drop. Lowest construction cost. Can be elevated to Level 2 or Level 3 later.
Medium dam. Holds 2 water drops. Can be built directly or by elevating a Level 1 dam.
Largest dam. Holds 3 water drops. Highest construction cost. Can be built directly or by elevating a Level 1 or Level 2 dam.
Upgrading an existing dam to a higher level. Costs the difference in machinery between levels. More efficient than building a new dam because the basin position is already secured.
A complete set of dam + conduit + powerhouse that can generate energy. All three structures must be connected. Incomplete networks produce nothing.
One of the two machinery types used for construction. Required for building dams, conduits, and powerhouses. Spent excavators enter the construction wheel.
One of the two machinery types used for construction. Required alongside excavators for building structures. Spent mixers enter the construction wheel.
Currency used to pay construction costs and certain action spaces. Earned during the income phase and from contract bonuses. Each unspent credit is worth 1 VP at game end.
Blue token representing water in the basins. Placed during water replenishment. Captured by dams, released during production, and flows downstream after use.
Worker token placed on action spaces to perform actions. The number available depends on player count. All engineers return at the start of each round.
Action space where engineers are placed to build dams, conduits, or powerhouses. Pay the required machinery and credits, then place the structure on the map.
Action space where engineers are placed to activate a production network. Release water from a dam through a conduit to a powerhouse. Energy = drops × conduit value.
Action space where engineers are placed to pick up a contract tile. Contracts must be acquired before production to be fulfilled during that production.
Action space where engineers are placed to acquire a technology tile. Pay the required cost for a permanent upgrade or special ability.
Action space where engineers contribute to a shared project for immediate VP or resources. Useful when machinery is locked in the construction wheel.
Action space allowing manipulation of water drops on the map. Place drops into basins with your dams or move water between connected basins.
A location on the map where dams can be built and water drops are held. Connected to other basins by river channels. Water flows from higher basins to lower ones.
The topmost basins on the map where new water drops are placed during water replenishment. Premium positions for dam placement.
After energy production, used water drops flow to the next lower basin. If an opponent has a dam there, they capture the water. Core mechanic that rewards downstream positioning.
First phase of each round. New water drops are placed in headwater basins according to the round track. The number and location of drops varies by round.
Connection between basins on the map. Determines the direction of water flow. Water always moves from higher basins to lower ones along these channels.

Rotating mechanism on each company board. Spent machinery is placed in the top segment. The wheel advances one segment per round. Machinery returns to available supply when it falls out of the bottom.
One compartment of the construction wheel. Segments vary in size. Machinery placed in a segment stays there until the wheel rotates enough for it to exit.
At the end of each round, every player's construction wheel rotates one segment. Machinery that exits the wheel returns to the player's available supply.
Machinery spent on construction returns in approximately 2-3 rounds depending on the wheel segment it enters. Planning builds around this timing is essential.
Asymmetric power tile chosen during setup. Each officer grants a unique ability that fundamentally changes how a company plays. Strategy should be built around the officer's power.
Player board showing structure storage, machinery supply, construction wheel, and income track. As structures are removed to build on the map, income symbols are revealed underneath.
Track on the main board measuring total energy produced. Determines turn order (lowest goes first) and contributes VP at game end. Advancing is good for points but bad for turn order.
Tile showing an energy production requirement and a reward. Committed before production. If production meets or exceeds the requirement, the contract is fulfilled for VP and bonuses.
Three tiles drawn at game start defining bonus scoring conditions. Evaluated at the end of certain rounds. Rewards things like most dams, most conduits, regional presence.
VP symbols revealed on the company board when structures are removed for building. More structures built = more bonuses revealed = higher end-game score.
Permanent upgrade acquired via the technology action space. Provides ongoing benefits: reduced construction costs, bonus production, or special abilities.
After 5 rounds: energy track VP + fulfilled contract VP + objective tile bonuses + building bonuses + remaining credits. Tiebreaker: higher position on energy track.
Determined by position on the energy track. The player lowest on the track goes first, giving them first pick of action spaces. Resets each round.
Second phase of each round. Players collect credits and bonuses shown on their company board. More structures built = more income symbols revealed.
Third phase of each round. Players take turns placing engineers on action spaces. Starts with the player lowest on the energy track. Continues until all players pass.
When a player has no more engineers to place or chooses to stop, they pass for the rest of the action phase. Once all players pass, the action phase ends.
Dam placed during setup in games with fewer than 4 players. Blocks certain basins to scale the map appropriately. Cannot be used by any player.
Track showing the current round (1-5) and the water replenishment configuration for each round. Determines how many water drops enter the map.
Number printed on a conduit indicating energy generated per water drop. Energy produced = water drops released × conduit production value.
Energy = number of water drops released from dam × production value of the conduit. Example: 3 drops × value 4 conduit = 12 energy.
